questionTypes.title: Synonyms
questionTypes.rule: Synonyms are words with the same or closest meaning. They must be the same word class.

practiceQuestions.stem: Find the synonym: ancient = ?
Find the synonym: quiet = ?
Find the synonym: rapid = ?
Choose the closest meaning: cautious = ?
Choose the closest meaning: reluctant = ?
Which word is a synonym of vital?

questionTypes.examTechnique: Say your own synonym first, then eliminate wrong word type and wrong strength.

traps.heading: Trap 1: wrong strength
traps.explanation: annoyed vs furious (not the same strength)

practiceQuestion.stem: Pick the closest synonym for cautious.

traps.heading: Trap 2: wrong word class
traps.explanation: adjective picked when the blank needs a verb

practiceQuestion.stem: Pick the closest synonym for ancient.

traps.heading: Trap 3: wrong meaning sense
traps.explanation: sharp=pointy vs sharp=clever

practiceQuestion.stem: Pick the closest synonym for assist.

traps.heading: Trap 4: related not same
traps.explanation: doctor and nurse are related, not synonyms

practiceQuestion.stem: Spot the odd one out: pleased, cheerful, delighted, furious.

traps.heading: Trap 5: ignore context
traps.explanation: sentence meaning changes with your choice

options: She was delighted to win the prize.
The rock was delighted in the field.
Delighted is a type of food.

highFrequencyWords.title: High-frequency synonym sets worth drilling for Bexley

classes.name: feelings
classes.examples: happy=pleased, cheerful, delighted; sad=unhappy, gloomy, miserable; scared=frightened, nervous, terrified

classes.name: good and bad
classes.examples: good=excellent, great, superb; bad=awful, terrible, poor; kind=caring, helpful, thoughtful

classes.name: size and amount
classes.examples: big=huge, enormous, massive; small=tiny, little, miniature; many=numerous, several, plenty

classes.name: speed and movement
classes.examples: fast=quick, rapid, swift; slow=sluggish, steady, gradual; run=sprint, dash, race

classes.name: thinking
classes.examples: think=believe, suppose, imagine; know=realise, recognise, be aware; understand=grasp, comprehend, figure out

classes.name: speaking and writing
classes.examples: say=state, tell, mention; shout=yell, scream, roar; reply=respond, answer, return

classes.name: time words
classes.examples: start=begin, commence, launch; finish=complete, end, conclude; early=soon, prompt, ahead of time

classes.name: school and learning
classes.examples: teach=educate, instruct, train; learn=study, practise, master; help=assist, support, aid

questionTypes.title: Antonyms
questionTypes.rule: Antonyms are words with opposite meaning. They must be the same word class.

practiceQuestions.stem: Find the antonym: valuable = ?
Find the antonym: permit = ?
Choose most opposite: intentional = ?
Choose most opposite: release = ?
Which word is an antonym of cautious?

questionTypes.examTechnique: Pick the pair that are most opposite, not just different. Re-read the question for choose two.

traps.heading: Trap 1: related not opposite
traps.explanation: careful is related to cautious, not opposite

traps.heading: Trap 2: wrong word class
traps.explanation: noun vs verb pair mixed

traps.heading: Trap 3: weak opposite
traps.explanation: good vs OK (not a true opposite)

traps.heading: Trap 4: wrong meaning sense
traps.explanation: light (not heavy) vs light (not dark)

practiceQuestion.stem: Odd one out: early, late, before, after, sandwich.

traps.heading: Trap 5: ignore context
traps.explanation: sentence meaning flips in an unexpected way

options: The rules forbid running in the corridor.

highFrequencyWords.title: High-frequency antonym pairs worth drilling for Bexley

questionTypes.title: Homonyms
questionTypes.rule: Homophones sound the same but have different spelling and meaning. Homographs have the same spelling but different meaning.

practiceQuestions.stem: Choose the correct homophone: I can ___ the music.
Choose the correct homophone: ___ going now.
Choose the correct homophone: I ___ an apple yesterday.
Choose the correct homophone: Please ___ for me.
Choose the correct word: The head teacher is the ___.
What are the two meanings of bank?
What are the two meanings of bat?
Which sentence uses they're correctly?

questionTypes.examTechnique: Decide the meaning first. Then choose the spelling. For they're, replace with they are to check.

traps.heading: Trap 1: choose by sound only
traps.explanation: pick the word you have seen before, not the one that fits

traps.heading: Trap 2: ignore grammar
traps.explanation: they're used where their is needed

traps.heading: Trap 3: mix up pair
traps.explanation: principal vs principle

traps.heading: Trap 4: miss homograph meaning
traps.explanation: close (near) vs close (shut)

practiceQuestion.stem: Which are two meanings of tear?

traps.heading: Trap 5: rush without reread
traps.explanation: not reading the full sentence again

highFrequencyWords.title: High-frequency homonyms worth drilling for Bexley

questionTypes.title: Adjectives
questionTypes.rule: Adjectives describe nouns. They answer what kind, how many, or which one.

practiceQuestions.stem: Which word is an adjective: brave knight?
Which word is an adjective: fierce dragon?
Which word is an adjective: younger sister?
Which word is an adjective: ancient castle?
Which word is an adjective: high hill?
Which adjective fits best: The ___ teacher explained the problem.
Which adjective fits best: A ___ storm destroyed the village.
Which adjective fits best: The ___ children played in the garden.

questionTypes.examTechnique: Match the describing word to the noun it modifies. Point to the noun, then check the adjective.

traps.heading: Trap 1: adjective vs adverb
traps.explanation: quiet vs quietly

practiceQuestion.stem: Which adjective fits: The ancient castle stood on the high hill. Which word is an adjective?

traps.heading: Trap 2: miss the noun
traps.explanation: the adjective describes a different noun than you think

traps.heading: Trap 3: ignore number words
traps.explanation: three, several, many are adjectives too

practiceQuestion.stem: Add one adjective: The ___ dog barked.

traps.heading: Trap 4: forget lists
traps.explanation: cold, dark night has 2 adjectives

practiceQuestion.stem: Spot an adjective in: a cold dark night.

traps.heading: Trap 5: confusing ed/ing
traps.explanation: bored child vs boring film

practiceQuestion.stem: Which sentence has two adjectives before one noun?

highFrequencyWords.title: High-frequency adjective banks worth drilling for Bexley

classes.name: colour
classes.examples: bright, dark, pale, golden, red, blue

questionTypes.title: Adverbs
questionTypes.rule: Adverbs modify verbs, adjectives, or other adverbs. They tell how, when, where, how often, or how much.

practiceQuestions.stem: Which word is an adverb: She sang beautifully.
Which word is an adverb: They arrived yesterday.
Which word is an adverb: The cat is outside.
Which word is an adverb: He always does his homework.
Which word is an adverb: The test was extremely difficult.
Which adverb of manner fits: He answered ___.
Which adverb of time fits: We will leave ___.
Which adverb of frequency fits: She ___ practises.

questionTypes.examTechnique: Ask: how, when, where, how often, how much. Many end in ly, but not all.

traps.heading: Trap 1: assume ly always
traps.explanation: friendly is an adjective, not an adverb

traps.heading: Trap 2: adjective vs adverb
traps.explanation: quiet vs quietly

practiceQuestion.stem: Spot the adverb: She arrived yesterday.

traps.heading: Trap 3: place in sentence
traps.explanation: putting the adverb in a place that changes meaning

practiceQuestion.stem: Add an adverb: The dog barked ___.

traps.heading: Trap 4: degree words
traps.explanation: very, extremely are adverbs of degree

traps.heading: Trap 5: well vs good
traps.explanation: I did well (not good)

highFrequencyWords.title: High-frequency adverbs worth drilling for Bexley

classes.name: manner
classes.examples: quickly, slowly, carefully, badly, well, neatly

classes.name: time
classes.examples: yesterday, today, soon, now, later, finally

classes.name: frequency
classes.examples: always, usually, often, sometimes, never, rarely

questionTypes.title: Word Relationships and Analogies
questionTypes.rule: Analogies compare relationships: A is to B as C is to D. Match the same relationship.

practiceQuestions.stem: Complete: bird:nest :: bee:?
Complete: knife:cut :: pen:?
Complete: winter:cold :: summer:?
Complete: sip:drink :: nibble:?
Complete: pupil:school :: patient:?
Complete: whisper:quiet :: shout:?

questionTypes.examTechnique: Say the relationship in words first. Then apply the same relationship to the second pair.

traps.heading: Trap 1: linked but wrong relationship
traps.explanation: both about school, but not the same relationship

practiceQuestion.stem: What is the relationship in doctor:hospital?

traps.heading: Trap 2: category vs example
traps.explanation: fruit:apple vs apple:fruit confusion

traps.heading: Trap 3: tool vs place mix
traps.explanation: chef:knife vs chef:kitchen

traps.heading: Trap 4: two rules at once
traps.explanation: mix place and purpose

traps.heading: Trap 5: grammar mismatch
traps.explanation: verb pair must stay verb pair

highFrequencyWords.title: High-frequency analogy patterns worth drilling for Bexley

questionTypes.title: Letter Codes (A1-Z26 and shifts)
questionTypes.rule: Letters can be coded as numbers (A1 to Z26) or shifted by a fixed move. Apply the same move to every letter.

practiceQuestions.stem: Shift +1: A becomes?
Shift +1: D becomes?
Shift +1: Y becomes?
A1-Z26: What is the code for DOG?
Shift -2: B becomes?
Decode 6-1-20 (A1-Z26)

questionTypes.examTechnique: Write the move (+ or -) before you start. Work one letter at a time in a neat row.

traps.heading: Trap 1: no wrap-around
traps.explanation: Z+1 is A, not a non-letter

traps.heading: Trap 2: move changes
traps.explanation: first letter +2, next letter +1 by mistake

traps.heading: Trap 3: wrong direction
traps.explanation: forward instead of backward

traps.heading: Trap 4: skip a letter
traps.explanation: jumping two letters when move is +1

traps.heading: Trap 5: misread instruction
traps.explanation: answer needed as letters but you leave numbers

highFrequencyWords.title: High-frequency code moves worth drilling for Bexley

classes.name: count letters
classes.examples: BOOK=4; PENCIL=6; vowels count; consonants count

questionTypes.title: Letter Sequences
questionTypes.rule: A sequence uses a repeated rule across positions: step size, alternating moves, or a cycle.

practiceQuestions.stem: Next letter: D, F, H, ?
Next letter: M, K, I, ?
Next letter: A, D, G, ?
Next letter: B, E, H, K, ?
Next letter: Z, W, T, Q, ?
Next letter: A, C, B, D, C, ?

questionTypes.examTechnique: Convert to A1-Z26 positions to spot the number pattern quickly.

traps.heading: Trap 1: assume too early
traps.explanation: rule fits first 2 terms but not all terms

traps.heading: Trap 2: miss alternation
traps.explanation: up then down pattern missed

traps.heading: Trap 3: miss cycle length
traps.explanation: A,B,C cycle missed

traps.heading: Trap 4: wrong letter positions
traps.explanation: thinking E is 6

traps.heading: Trap 5: no wrap-around check
traps.explanation: sequence may pass Z back to A

highFrequencyWords.title: High-frequency sequence rules worth drilling for Bexley

classes.name: position-based
classes.examples: prime positions; square positions mapped to letters

questionTypes.title: Anagrams and Word Building
questionTypes.rule: Anagrams rearrange letters to make a new word. Word building may add, remove, or swap letters to form a real word.

practiceQuestions.stem: Anagram of EAST = ?
Change one letter in COLD to make a word.
Add one letter to TONE to make a word.
Anagram of SPARE = ?
Remove one letter from PLATE to make a word.
Change two letters in LATE to make a new word.

questionTypes.examTechnique: Sort vowels and consonants, then build using common endings like ed, ing, er.

traps.heading: Trap 1: missing a letter
traps.explanation: you forget one letter from the set

traps.heading: Trap 2: repeat a letter
traps.explanation: you use a letter twice

traps.heading: Trap 3: not a real word
traps.explanation: looks plausible but not a word

practiceQuestion.stem: Add a letter to TONE to make a word.

traps.heading: Trap 4: ignore common chunks
traps.explanation: missing endings like ing or ed

traps.heading: Trap 5: spelling not checked
traps.explanation: word built but misspelt

highFrequencyWords.title: High-frequency anagram and word-build patterns worth drilling for Bexley

classes.name: common endings
classes.examples: ed, ing, er, est, tion, ly

classes.name: double letters
classes.examples: need, pool, letter patterns like ll, ss, oo

questionTypes.title: Hidden Words
questionTypes.rule: A hidden word is formed by consecutive letters inside a longer string or across a word break. It may run forwards or backwards.

practiceQuestions.stem: Find the hidden word: stoNE ARMy
Find the hidden word: thE CAT Is
Find the hidden word: piLOT ONly
Find the hidden word: suGAR DEN
Find a hidden word across two words: close train
Find the backwards hidden word in: emit

questionTypes.examTechnique: Scan with your finger in chunks of 3 to 6 letters. Check forwards then backwards.

traps.heading: Trap 1: skipping letters
traps.explanation: taking letters from different places

traps.heading: Trap 2: not consecutive
traps.explanation: hidden words must be consecutive

traps.heading: Trap 3: forget backwards
traps.explanation: backwards option missed

traps.heading: Trap 4: stop too soon
traps.explanation: you see a 2-letter word and stop

traps.heading: Trap 5: wrong length
traps.explanation: question asks 4 letters but you found 3

practiceQuestion.stem: Find a 3-letter hidden word in: station.

highFrequencyWords.title: High-frequency hidden-word patterns worth drilling for Bexley

classes.name: across two words
classes.examples: close train -> set; note about -> tea

classes.name: scan routine
classes.examples: move 1 letter along each time and re-check

questionTypes.title: Odd One Out (Words)
questionTypes.rule: Three words share one clear, provable property. One word does not share it.

practiceQuestions.stem: Odd one out: chair, table, sofa, carrot
Odd one out: whisper, shout, scream, spoon
Odd one out: London, Paris, Rome, Tuesday
Odd one out: generous, kind, helpful, selfish
Odd one out: triangle, square, circle, running
Odd one out: early, late, before, sandwich

questionTypes.examTechnique: State the shared category for the three, then check all three fit before choosing.

traps.heading: Trap 1: choose unknown word
traps.explanation: pick the word you do not know

practiceQuestion.stem: Odd one out: apple, banana, grape, hammer.

traps.heading: Trap 2: use two rules
traps.explanation: meaning plus spelling at the same time

practiceQuestion.stem: Odd one out: run, jump, swim, blue.

traps.heading: Trap 3: opinion based
traps.explanation: I do not like it, so it is odd

practiceQuestion.stem: Odd one out: London, Paris, Rome, Tuesday.

traps.heading: Trap 4: miss word class
traps.explanation: one is a verb, others are nouns

practiceQuestion.stem: Odd one out: generous, kind, helpful, selfish.

traps.heading: Trap 5: do not prove
traps.explanation: no clear property stated

practiceQuestion.stem: What is the rule for odd one out: cat, dog, fish, apple?

highFrequencyWords.title: High-frequency odd-one-out categories worth drilling for Bexley

questionTypes.title: Cloze (Best Word in a Sentence)
questionTypes.rule: Choose the word that best completes the sentence. Use meaning, grammar, and connectives to decide.
examples: Despite the rain, we still played
Although it was late, we stayed calm

practiceQuestions.stem: Choose: I was tired, ___ I finished the work.
Choose: The dog barked ___.
Choose: She was ___ to help.
Choose: ___ it was late, we stayed.
Choose: He spoke ___ during the test.
Choose: The plan was ___ perfect.

questionTypes.examTechnique: Cover options and predict the missing word first. Then choose the option that matches word type and meaning.

traps.heading: Trap 1: grammar wrong
traps.explanation: quiet chosen when quietly is needed

traps.heading: Trap 2: connector clue missed
traps.explanation: although vs because signals different meaning

practiceQuestion.stem: Choose: ___ it was raining, we went.

traps.heading: Trap 3: tense mismatch
traps.explanation: yesterday needs past tense

traps.heading: Trap 4: fancy word trap
traps.explanation: pick the longest word

traps.heading: Trap 5: no reread
traps.explanation: not reading sentence with your choice

options: Although it rained, we went out.

highFrequencyWords.title: High-frequency cloze patterns worth drilling for Bexley

classes.name: connectives
classes.examples: because, although, however, therefore, despite, unless

classes.name: comparatives
classes.examples: bigger, smaller, more, most, less, least

classes.name: pronouns
classes.examples: he, she, they, him, her, them

questionTypes.title: Word Class Basics (Noun, Verb, Adjective, Adverb)
questionTypes.rule: Nouns name, verbs do, adjectives describe nouns, adverbs describe verbs or adjectives.

practiceQuestions.stem: What word class is 'fox' in 'The quick fox ran'?
What word class is 'softly' in 'She whispered softly'?
What word class is 'planned' in 'They planned a trip'?
Choose the correct form: He spoke ___.
Choose the correct form: It was a ___ decision.
Choose the correct form: She smiled ___.

questionTypes.examTechnique: Use the sentence frame. If a blank needs a verb, do not choose a noun.

traps.heading: Trap 1: ly confusion
traps.explanation: friendly looks like an adverb but is an adjective

traps.heading: Trap 2: adjective vs adverb
traps.explanation: quiet vs quietly

practiceQuestion.stem: Which is the adjective: The ancient castle stood.

traps.heading: Trap 3: noun vs verb form
traps.explanation: plan vs planned

practiceQuestion.stem: Which is the verb: They sprinted home.

traps.heading: Trap 4: agreement
traps.explanation: they were vs he was

traps.heading: Trap 5: meaning changes
traps.explanation: word fits grammar but changes meaning

practiceQuestion.stem: Which sentence uses an adverb of time?

highFrequencyWords.title: High-frequency word class items worth drilling for Bexley

classes.name: nouns
classes.examples: teacher, city, idea, team, problem, answer

classes.name: verbs
classes.examples: run, build, decide, explain, improve, solve

classes.name: adjectives
classes.examples: brave, calm, anxious, huge, tiny, ancient

classes.name: adverbs
classes.examples: quickly, carefully, quietly, slowly, neatly, honestly

classes.name: pronouns
classes.examples: he, she, they, we, them, us

classes.name: connectives
classes.examples: because, although, while, if, when, since

classes.name: prepositions
classes.examples: under, over, between, through, across, within

classes.name: determiners
classes.examples: a, an, the, this, that, these, those
